speaker: interviewer
text: --two with Klaus Ollendorff. You finished the last tape with Hitler coming into power in 1933. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the day exactly. It was January 30th, 1933. And my mother and I came back from a visit in the early afternoon and passed a kiosk. And the headline on the papers were 'Hitler Appointed Chancellor.' And we felt troubled. But we continued talking about the things we had talked about before. And when we sat down for dinner-- my father had come home-- we again mentioned the fact that Hitler had been appointed chancellor. And my sister said, well, it can't be so terrible because Thuringen-- that's Thuringia, which is a province of Prussia-- no, of Saxony-- has a Nazi prime minister and nothing has happened there which is particularly upsetting. And then we all went on with our normal conversations and our normal tasks. My father took his brief nap and went back to the office. My sister and I did our homework. She diligently, I reluctantly. And in the afternoon we did what we always did, I'm sure. We went to the bakery and got a freshly-baked roll for the afternoon-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] vesper, it was called. Vesper is the Latin word for afternoon, also the Italian word for afternoon. The afternoon meal was called vesper-- V-E-S-P-E-R. There's an opera too, by Verdi. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then life went on quite normally for a few hours until either that-- oh, yeah. That evening I was glued to the radio. Each one of us had a radio in our room. And I listened to the military music which was played during the torchlight parade for Hitler in Berlin. It was on the [? Potsdam ?] Street, [? Potsdam ?] [? Strasse. ?] And I loved military music. And then I listened to Hitler's speech, which I often imitated. I don't know that I did it well, but I thought so. And he said, one year of social democracy has [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] disturbed Germany and one year of Bolshevism would have ruined it forever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In German it sounded like this, as spoken by Hitler, [GERMAN]. Subsequently, a few days thereafter, the Reichstag Building, the parliament building, burned. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A fire had been set there and the building really was destroyed. And five people, the arsonists, the suspected arsonists, were arrested. Two were Bulgarian communists with names I forgot, but they're mentioned in many books, the leader of the German Communist Party, a man by the name of Torgler, another person whose name I forgot, and a Dutch communist by the name of Van de Lubbe And Van de Lubbe was apprehended. And his picture was shown. He was in the Reichstag Building. He had a torch in his hand. And he readily confessed to having set fire to the Reichstag. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I mean, everybody knew he had been drugged. And he had been promised all kinds of things, that he wouldn't be arrested. But he became the focus of the investigation and of the trial. And a lengthy trial was held to show how fair the Nazis are. And he was given a defense counsel. And I think the defense counsel was a rather prominent lawyer. But, needless to say, he was found guilty and he was executed. And the other, the three Bulgarians-- the two Bulgarians and the man I can't remember were acquitted and they were asked to leave Germany. And Torgler, the communist, was also found not guilty. But immediately thereafter he was arrested by the Gestapo and taken to a concentration camp where he later on died. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Everybody knew that this was staged and it was a lie. But it gave Hitler the opportunity to go before Congress, before the Parliament, and ask it to outlaw the Communist party in a democratic way. And the vote was sufficiently in favor of outlawing the party. And the Communist Party was outlawed. It would be like here some bill being introduced to outlaw the Green Party, you know, if it had any seats already. And I think if they could get enough votes together to outlaw the Green Party. So this was a democratic process, again, in quotes. And Hitler said now we will hold another election to see what the Germans really want. And this time it was without the Communist party being on the ballot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was also rumored quite a bit that the ballot would not be very secret. And many people were afraid to vote social democratic, which was still on the ticket, on the ballot. And, needless to say, the Nazi party won by a landslide. And then parliament was reconvened and a bill was again introduced to outlaw all parties except the Nazi parties. And this bill was approved by Parliament. And then Germany had a one-party system, the Nazis. And that was just a formality. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] By that time, we all began to be rather scared of what will happen next. But nobody really thought that anything like the Holocaust would happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this is still early--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's early. First year, 1933.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1933 my school immediately started an anti-aircraft brigade. And I joined it. We went on exercises near the city to shoot down Polish planes. That was always-- we were so close to the Polish border. And we were taught, you know, how to lie down without being seen and keep our heels down when we lie down. And I enjoyed that a lot. I was the only Jew. And I don't think anybody paid any attention to the fact that-- because, after all, they thought that if we are attacked by the Poles, I would suffer as much as they do. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1933-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What was happening in school then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I mean, one teacher was a wonderful man who had enough courage to say to one of the kids who already came dressed in the Hitler Youth shirt, and he didn't know the answer to a certain question, and he says why don't you become a stable boy for Hitler instead of coming to school? You're wasting your time. In other words, there was not enough fear yet, you know, for a teacher to make a remark like this. This was in 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what school were you at then?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was in a gymnasium, which is the equivalent of high school here. I started that at age 9 and 3/4. And I left the school three years because before the official graduation in order to get a graduation certificate from another school, a commercial high school, which would entitle me to enter a university and get a degree, a PhD. Everything was PhD. I don't think they had master's degrees in business administration and commerce economics and so on. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but the last-- I stayed in my first school until 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klaus, Hitler Youth you mentioned. Were friends of yours joining the movement? Were there rallies in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were rallies.

speaker: interviewer
text: You traveled back and forth to Berlin because of the family. What were you seeing? Where there differences in the two cities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Berlin was a much more liberal city. Berlin had the greatest number of drama critics, Jews, film directors. Some of the most famous directors work for the UFA, U-F-A. And they, ultimately, came to America. I don't know whether the name Ernst Lubitsch means anything to you. We want to Berlin very often to visit the grandparents. I always felt very comfortable there. And at that time, instead of worrying more about the Nazis, we collected autographs. And since Elisabeth Bergner lived only three blocks from my grandfather's house, we kept pestering her for autographs. We always bought these black and white photographs and hoped--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe who was Elisabeth Bergner.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the most famous actress in Germany. And she ultimately came to America, where she became prominent but not so famous. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of the famous actors you may not know any because you're all too young. You should have sent octogenarians here. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know the man who wrote the background music for Steiner, he was an Austrian refugee. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did you ever see Peter Lorre?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was from Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, Berlin was wonderful. And in Berlin there was less prejudice than anyplace else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although it was the seat of government. It was the capital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is the capital.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- was the capital. But they still had cabarets as late as 1935, you know, where they-- I went to one where they made tremendous jokes about Hitler. And were not closed or arrested. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember one of those comedians said something against the Nazis. And then they closed it. And when he reopened two days later, he said first I was too open and then they closed me for being too open. So now I don't know what to do. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Berlin had wonderful museums. Still does. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So your life was not substantially affected or affected at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it really wasn't. And that's why I can understand that my parents were very reluctant to make certain preparations to leave. My uncle who was a radiologist, Dr. Cohen, was married to my mother's sister, decided to leave Germany in 1934 because his daughter had joined after the Nazis came to power the German socialist students union. And after she did that, the Gestapo came to their house and did a house search to find out whether they had subversive material. And that house search scared them so, the parents, that they decided they better leave as soon as possible. And at that time my uncle received an invitation from Mount Sinai Hospital in Chicago, which had only a visiting sort of radiology department. That means they didn't have a full-time radiologist. Offered him a job to become the head of a new radiology department at Mount Sinai. And my uncle grabbed this opportunity. And he came here and he accepted it. And he started that x-ray department. And he brought some wonderful refugees with him to assist him. He had only one arm. He had lost one arm in World War I. He had been a surgeon before and switched to radiology, roentgenology, because he couldn't operate anymore. He brought some wonderful roentgenologists with him. One was a Dr. [? Denelius ?]. And the other one was a Dr. Ahrendt, A-H-R-E-N-D-T, who had left Germany also and became the personal physician to the Shah of Iran. But he didn't because he accepted that position because it got him out of Germany and also because he liked to riot. But then he didn't like it with the Shah. It wasn't his kind of Shah, quoting somebody who was Kennedy's assistant who once said that about the Shah of Iran. And he became his number two man here at Mount Sinai.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents respond? How did your mother respond to her sister moving?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is-- nothing. She was going to miss her and her brother-in-law. But she didn't see such urgency. She said, you didn't belong to any socialist groups. You know, they had to leave because they were under surveillance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anti-Semitic propaganda, anti-Semitic films for instance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not films yet. But I saw a lot of graffiti. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In '33, not so much. In '34, a lot of graffiti. And then in '34, when we went on summer vacation, we went to a Czechoslovakian resort because there we could get foreign newspapers and be free of temporary fear. But it makes no sense to me in retrospect, you know, that one would go for two weeks to another country to breathe the air of freedom, you know, and then go back where you-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where policy was formed which-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] from which you could anticipate that the Jews would be deprived. And Hitler had written It was his biography, his plan. He wrote that when he was in prison. And I had read And in he had said that the Jews have to leave Germany. Germany must be free of Jews. The word was Judenfrei, Judenrein. And it said, we will not bend one hair of a Jew, but we'll make it impossible for them to exist. They will not have jobs. They will not have businesses. They will not have professions. They will perish from attrition. And he did it so gradually that many people didn't take it seriously. The Nuremberg Laws, which came in 1935-- it was my last summer in Breslau, in Germany-- a law came that the Jews could not have female Aryan household help that is older than-- younger than 45. And I think our cook was over 45. We could keep her. And she felt insulted by the law. That Hitler would think that a woman over 45 is no longer attractive enough for a young Jew to rape her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As one looks back on it, you know, you cannot believe why anybody stayed, anybody who had the opportunity to leave. But they did. And I often think if there were any signals like this here in Chicago, that I would be as unaffected by it as my parents were. I don't think I would want to leave my house and start, you know, learn a new language and start all over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say you don't think you would?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you understand their reticence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you, you said you had a friend. You had mentioned you had a friend in school who said to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: He said in 1934 you better leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [? Gunthe ?] Grimke. Grimke, G-R-I-M-K-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A classmate.

speaker: interviewer
text: --part of the Hitler Youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- yes. He was a Hitler Youth. He wore that little arm band. Not always. But I had a farewell party before I left to the United States. There were mostly Gentiles at that party, my friends. And some people said to me, you're crazy to leave. But [? Gunthe ?] Grimke said you're doing the right thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you need to convince your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. Not much because the general feeling in Germany was that all the people thought the children must leave. The older people can stay here. And the [GERMAN] but [INAUDIBLE]. My mother was 45 or 44 years old. No, they didn't opposed to it at all. My father would have preferred that I go to Switzerland and get a PhD in Lausanne because that's where he had studied. And he spoke French moderately well, with a heavy accent but fluently. And he would have preferred that because he was disappointed that I had not gotten the regular graduation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what happened with school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went then to this other school from which I got a graduation certificate. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you knew that you were going to be leaving or because you thought you might still--

speaker: interviewee
text: That I might leave. That I might leave. And the reason I didn't go to Switzerland is that I was afraid, and my father agreed with it, that he would not be able to send me money to stay there, you know. And Switzerland wasn't very anxious to give work permits to people. So if I wouldn't have any money to pay tuition and to live there or to live there and survive, it was too uncertain because of foreign currency laws which began to be developed in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would not have enabled your father to send you and pay tuition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for the first year he might have.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was that decision made? And was your sister living at home at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister had come back from America and she was living at home. And she wanted to stay in Germany longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to ask her that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was sort of half looking forward to coming to America. And even as a child, little child, I often wondered what America is like. When I looked out of the window I thought, Oh America! Now it's night there when it's day here and vice versa. And I pictured all these skyscrapers which I saw on boxes and letters. I was half curious and half afraid. But I'm very glad that my father did push the immigration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak English at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had high school English, which was enough to get around but not enough to get a job, a real job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made the actual arrangements for your traveling to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They decided how much clothes and how much-- what I should take with me to America. And my aunt had misinformed them and told them that sundry items and drugs and toothpaste and all this-- toilet paper-- is extremely expensive in America. And I came with, I think, eight large trunks. And two of them were packed with toilet paper and toothbrushes and toothpaste and all that kind of stuff. But, in addition, to that, since my father had a tremendous fear of my catching syphilis, he also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put hundreds of condoms in the suitcase. And when I arrived here, you know, he often wrote to me. And he said I hope you use condoms. But he didn't know that I was so shy I didn't use condoms. I didn't even close to any suggestions where I might be-- [LAUGHS] might be able to use them. And very often he signed his letters by saying never without. That meant never do it without it. Little did he know I didn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But a friend of mine in Trenton, New Jersey-- Let's not get that far ahead yet. Let's stay, please, in Germany preparing for the trip. You packed yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the packing supervised?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm sure my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the government interfere in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no restriction to what you could bring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. You couldn't take out any money. You could--

speaker: interviewer
text: How much money could you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot now. I think it was $100. But my parents were able to send me $10 a month, which I picked up at the post office in wherever I was, Trenton or Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you needed to have a sponsor in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a sponsor. First it was a second cousin of my father's. But it turned out that their financial condition wasn't good enough. And so I couldn't get a visa. And then a cousin of my mother's got his grandmother, Mrs. Steinhart, to give a secondary affidavit. And then I was given the visa for the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned to me that your mother had actually made a trip to the United States, or both your parents had made a trip to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only my mother had made a trip in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had been the purpose of her visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: To visit her sister. Her sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her sister from Berlin who had come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '35. Of course she came back. And it didn't occur to her to leave Germany even then. And then they came-- maybe I should tell that later, but my parents came to visit again in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, let's stay in the earlier part first. You made plans to leave. And what was your itinerary?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Berlin to Hook van Holland. And then I took the ferry from Hook van Holland to Harwich in England. And then I stayed in England three days or so, or four. And then I left from Liverpool.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter, or did your parents encounter, any difficulty in getting the travel documents that you needed from the government? The necessary immigration, visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only from the American consulate. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Only from the American--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, because the first affidavit wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: --sufficiently strong financially.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the leave taking of your family like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was sad. It was very sad. The last night I asked my father to sleep in the same room with me. We stayed in my grandfather's house in Berlin. And he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your mother come with you as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She stayed in another room. And my father rubbed my back, I remember. And gave council, sort of like Polonius to his son. You know, be honorable and be strong. Never beggar. Don't give loans. Don't be a lender. And things like this. But he was very nice to me. At the railroad station he cried. His eyes were very moist. And I don't know whether I cried or not at that moment, but I was scared. But compared to what other refugees had to suffer going on child transports to England, you know, be separated from their parents and just put on a train and sent to a foreign country and stay with people in homes, you know, where everybody was a stranger, that must've been very scary. I had it quite good. And so compared to what other people had to go through--

speaker: interviewer
text: The tape is nearly running out. So let's continue in tape three.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.
